title: Contrast and Resolution
narrationScript: Everything you detect in visual testing, you detect as contrast. A discontinuity does not announce itself. It simply sends slightly different light to your eye than the surface around it does. A little darker, a little brighter, a slightly different color. This lesson is about where that difference comes from, and how you can make it bigger on purpose. Start with reflection. When light hits a smooth, polished surface, it reflects specularly, like a mirror. The angle of reflection equals the angle of incidence, both measured from the normal, the imaginary line standing perpendicular to the surface. All the light goes one way. Stand in that one direction and the surface blazes. Stand anywhere else and it looks dark. A rough or textured surface reflects diffusely. Its microscopic hills and valleys scatter light in every direction, so the surface looks about equally bright from anywhere. Most real surfaces, machined steel, weld caps, blasted plate, are a mixture. A specular component rides on a diffuse component. That mixture is why a weld cap can flash at you from one position and go flat gray from another, and it is why moving your light, or your head, is a legitimate inspection technique and not fidgeting. Next, the law of illumination. From a small source, illuminance falls with the square of distance. Double the distance and the same light spreads over four times the area, so the surface gets one quarter the lux. Triple it, one ninth. There is also a tilt effect. When the beam strikes at an angle instead of straight on, it smears across more area, and illuminance drops with the cosine of the angle from the normal. Sixty degrees off normal costs you half. Those two effects together explain why a bore, a nozzle, or the far side of a fillet can be starved for light while the meter reads fine on the open plate beside it. Verify the light where the job is hardest, not where it is easiest. Now follow the light to your eye. Illuminance is the light arriving at the surface. Reflectivity is the fraction the surface sends back. Fresh white paint returns most of it. Dark oxide and soot return almost none. Luminance is the light actually leaving the surface toward your eye, and luminance is the only thing the eye sees. Luminous contrast is the difference in luminance between the indication and its background, compared against that background. A crack packed with dark oxide on bright machined steel is high contrast. The same crack on dark mill scale is nearly invisible. Under ideal conditions a trained eye can pick up a luminance difference of a few percent. In the field, with vibration and fatigue and imperfect light, plan on needing several times that. Raising illuminance does not change the contrast ratio, but it moves your eye into a range where it discriminates better, right up to the point where glare takes over. Contrast is also why cleanliness is a lighting topic. A film of oil, a layer of dust, gray primer overspray. Each one pulls the brightness of indication and background toward the same value and buries the difference you are hunting. Cleaning before examination is contrast recovery. Clean deliberately, though. Aggressive blasting can smear metal over a tight crack, and sometimes the stain is itself the indication, so look first and document what you found. Color is a second channel of contrast. Red-brown rust on gray steel. Blue and straw heat tint on stainless. When luminance contrast is weak, color difference can still flag the indication. But color vision lives in the cones, and cones need bright light and an honest, full-spectrum source. Under dim light or a poor color-rendering lamp, color contrast disappears. Then there is the technique that feels like a magic trick, dark-field contrast. Bring your light in at a low, grazing angle across a relatively smooth surface. The smooth field reflects the beam away from your eye, so the background goes dark. But a scratch, a raised edge of spatter, the rim of a dent, anything that breaks the surface, scatters light upward into your eye. The indication glows bright against a dark field. The same grazing geometry makes surface relief throw long shadows, so undercut, mismatch, and dents stand out, and shadow length even hints at depth. That is surface geometry working for you, and it is why one fixed light position is never enough. Finally, resolution. With twenty-twenty vision you resolve about one minute of arc. At twenty-four inches, that is a feature around two tenths of a millimeter. But that number assumes good contrast. As contrast falls, the smallest thing you can see grows. Light, contrast, and resolution are one system. Control the first two, and the third takes care of itself.

sections.heading: Reflection at Smooth and Rough Surfaces
sections.body:
When light strikes a surface, what happens next depends on how rough that surface is compared with the wavelength of the light.

A **smooth, polished surface** reflects specularly — like a mirror. The angle of reflection equals the angle of incidence, both measured from the normal, the imaginary line perpendicular to the surface at the point of strike. All the reflected energy travels in one direction. Stand in that direction and the surface blazes; stand anywhere else and it can look almost black.

A **rough or textured surface** reflects diffusely. Its microscopic hills and valleys present every possible local angle, so the incoming beam scatters across the whole hemisphere above the surface. A truly matte surface looks about equally bright from every viewing position.

Nearly every surface you inspect — machined faces, weld caps, blasted plate, mill scale — is a **mixture**: a specular lobe riding on a diffuse spread. That is why a weld cap flashes at you from one position and goes flat gray from another. It is also why deliberately moving the lamp, or your head, is a core technique rather than a nervous habit: each geometry sends a different sample of the surface's light to your eye, and each reveals different features.

sections.caption: Smooth surfaces reflect specularly (angle in equals angle out, measured from the normal), rough surfaces scatter diffusely, and real surfaces do both at once.

sections.heading: The Law of Illumination
sections.body:
How much light lands on the surface follows two simple geometric rules, together called the law of illumination.

**Inverse square.** Light from a small source spreads as it travels, so illuminance falls with the square of distance: E = I/d². Double the lamp-to-surface distance and the same flux covers four times the area — one quarter the lux. A lamp that delivers 1000 lx at half a meter delivers 250 lx at one meter and about 111 lx at a meter and a half. This is why holding the flashlight at arm's length instead of up close can silently drop you below the procedure minimum.

**Cosine law.** Illuminance also falls as the beam strikes at a slant: E = (I/d²)·cos θ, where θ is measured from the normal. At 60° off normal, cos θ = 0.5 — half the light per unit area, because the beam smears across a longer footprint.

Combine the two and you see why bores, nozzle interiors, and the far sides of fillets are light-starved even when the meter reads fine on the open plate nearby — distance grows and incidence flattens at the same time. Verify illuminance at the worst real location, not the easiest one.

sections.caption: Illuminance falls with the square of distance from a small source, and with the cosine of the angle at which the beam strikes the surface.

sections.heading: Reflectivity, Luminance, and Luminous Contrast
sections.body:
Follow the light all the way to your eye and three quantities chain together.

**Reflectivity** is the fraction of incident light a surface returns. Rough working values: fresh white paint returns around 80 percent; clean machined steel returns roughly half, much of it specular; dark oxide, mill scale, and soot can return well under 10 percent.

**Luminance** is the light actually leaving the surface toward your eye — the brightness you perceive. For a diffuse surface it scales with illuminance times reflectivity: light the surface better, or make it return more, and luminance rises.

**Luminous contrast** is what detection runs on: the difference in luminance between the indication and its background, judged against the background — C = (L_indication − L_background) / L_background. An oxide-filled crack on bright machined steel is high contrast; the same crack crossing dark mill scale is nearly invisible because both luminances are low and close together.

Two practical consequences. First, raising illuminance does not change the contrast ratio — both luminances rise proportionally — but it moves the eye into an adaptation range where it discriminates small differences better, up to the glare point. Under ideal conditions a trained eye detects a luminance difference of a few percent; in field conditions, plan on needing several times that. Second, contrast sets resolution. With 20/20 acuity you resolve about one arc-minute — roughly 0.18 mm at the standard 600 mm viewing distance — but only at good contrast. As contrast falls, the smallest feature you can actually see grows, and magnification becomes the honest fix.

sections.heading: Cleanliness and Color: Two Contrast Channels
sections.body:
**Cleanliness is contrast.** A thin oil film, a coat of dust, gray primer overspray, smeared metal from aggressive grinding — every one of them pulls the luminance of indication and background toward the same value, burying the difference you are hunting. Cleaning before examination is not housekeeping; it is contrast recovery. Degrease, brush, and wipe to the level your procedure requires, and be deliberate about methods: harsh blasting or peening can smear metal over tight surface-breaking indications and hide them from every method that follows. And sometimes the foreign material — a rust bloom, a weep stain — is itself the indication, so look before you clean and document the as-found condition when the procedure calls for it.

**Color is the second channel.** When luminance contrast is weak, a difference in color can still flag the indication: red-brown rust on gray steel, straw-to-blue heat-tint bands on stainless, coating discoloration over hot spots. Two conditions must hold for color contrast to work. The light must be bright enough for cone vision — color discrimination collapses at low light levels — and the source must render color honestly, which means a full-spectrum, high-CRI lamp. Under high-pressure sodium or a poor LED, the tint bands you are being paid to judge simply are not there to see.

sections.heading: Dark-Field Contrast and Surface Geometry
sections.body:
Now the technique that exploits everything above: **dark-field contrast**. Bring the light in at a low, grazing angle — a few degrees up to perhaps fifteen — across a relatively smooth surface. The smooth field reflects the beam specularly, onward and away from your eye, so the background appears dark. But anything that interrupts the surface — a scratch, a raised spatter edge, the lip of a dent, the displaced edges of a tight crack — scatters light out of the beam, and some of that scatter reaches your eye. The indication glows bright against a dark field. The smoother and cleaner the field, the more dramatic the effect, which is why dark-field work pairs naturally with the cleaning discipline above.

The same grazing geometry recruits **surface relief**. Raised features cast shadows away from the light; recessed features — undercut at a weld toe, a pit, a dent — hold shadow inside their down-light edge. Shadow length grows with feature height or depth, so a sweeping light gives you a live, qualitative depth gauge. This is why one fixed lighting position is never sufficient: rotate the light around the part, or the part under the light, and watch what appears and disappears.

Curved surfaces add one more effect: on a pipe or shell, geometry puts a bright specular band where the mirror condition is met and darker zones elsewhere. Work the examination zone through the favorable band rather than fighting it.

sections.caption: Grazing light darkens a smooth field while surface-breaking features scatter light to the eye, and directional light turns relief — undercut, dents, beads — into readable shadows.

sections.title: Make the Contrast — Don't Hope for It
sections.body:
Before calling a surface clear, run the levers:

- **Clean it** — films, dust, and overspray bury luminance differences.
- **Light it** — meet the lux minimum at the worst spot; remember inverse-square and cosine losses.
- **Kill the glare** — get eye and lamp off the specular angle.
- **Rake it** — grazing light for bright-on-dark indications and relief shadows.
- **Use color** — high-CRI light at photopic levels when luminance contrast is thin.
- **Move** — the light, your head, or the part. A feature invisible in one geometry is obvious in another.

interactive.title: Contrast Builders and Contrast Killers
interactive.intro: Classify each setup choice by its effect on the visibility of a surface indication.

items.label: Solvent-wipe the oil film off the surface before viewing
items.why: Films pull indication and background luminance together; cleaning restores the difference. Cleanliness is contrast recovery.

items.label: Hold the lamp at a grazing angle so the smooth field reflects the beam away from your eye
items.why: Dark-field technique: the field goes dark while scratches, spatter edges, and crack lips scatter light to the eye and glow bright.

items.label: Aim the lamp so its beam bounces off the shiny weld cap straight into your eye
items.why: That is reflected glare at the mirror angle — a veiling hot spot that washes out retinal contrast exactly where you are looking.

items.label: Leave light-gray primer overspray across the weld toe
items.why: Overspray evens out the luminance across the surface and can bridge tight indications, hiding exactly the difference you need to see.

items.label: Pick a high-CRI white source when judging heat-tint colors
items.why: Color contrast only works if the source contains the wavelengths the surface can reflect — a full-spectrum, high-CRI lamp keeps the color channel alive.

items.label: Inspect dark corrosion staining on dark mill scale under dim light
items.why: Both luminances are low and nearly equal, and at low light the cones quit — luminance contrast and color contrast both collapse.

items.label: Sweep the light around the part so shadows move across surface features
items.why: Directional light turns relief into shadows; sweeping it makes raised and recessed features appear, lengthen, and shift so the eye catches them.

items.label: Double the lamp-to-surface distance without re-checking the meter
items.why: Inverse square law: twice the distance means one quarter the illuminance — the eye drops into a poorer discrimination range and may fall below the procedure minimum.

checkQuestions.question: Light strikes a polished surface at 40° from the normal. The specular reflection leaves at:
options: 40° from the normal, on the opposite side of it
50° from the normal, on the same side
80° measured from the surface
Straight back along the incident ray
checkQuestions.explanation: The law of reflection: angle of reflection equals angle of incidence, both measured from the normal — so 40° in means 40° out on the other side of the normal.

checkQuestions.question: A lamp delivers 800 lx at 0.5 m. Moved back to 1.0 m with the same aim, the surface receives about:
options: 400 lx
200 lx
600 lx
100 lx
checkQuestions.explanation: Illuminance follows the inverse square law: doubling distance cuts it to one quarter — 800 ÷ 4 = 200 lx.

checkQuestions.question: Luminous contrast is best described as:
options: The difference between a lamp's lumen rating and the lux it produces
The luminance difference between an indication and its background, judged relative to the background
The ratio of illuminance at two different lamp distances
The difference in color rendering index between two light sources
checkQuestions.explanation: C = (L_indication − L_background) / L_background. Detection runs on this relative luminance difference — not on raw lamp output.

checkQuestions.question: Under grazing (dark-field) lighting, a fine scratch on a smooth plate appears bright because:
options: The scratch reflects specularly into the eye while the field scatters light away
The smooth field reflects the beam away from the eye while the scratch scatters light toward it
The scratch is at a higher temperature than the surrounding metal
Grazing light increases the illuminance landing on the scratch
checkQuestions.explanation: The smooth field obeys mirror geometry and sends the grazing beam past the observer, going dark; the scratch breaks the surface and scatters light upward into the eye, glowing bright on the dark field.

checkQuestions.question: With 20/20 vision at the standard 600 mm direct-viewing distance and good contrast, the smallest resolvable detail is on the order of:
options: 0.2 mm
2 mm
5 mm
checkQuestions.explanation: 20/20 acuity resolves about one arc-minute, roughly 0.3 milliradian; at 600 mm that is about 0.18 mm — and the limit grows as contrast falls.

references: ANSI/ASNT CP-105-2024, VT Level II Topical Outline §4.1–4.8
Codes such as ASME BPVC Section V, Article 9 (direct visual examination practice, cited generically)
Inverse-square and cosine laws of illumination — standard illumination-engineering relationships (original wording)
